---
title: "Worked example: coastal development and runoff"
---

## Example: understanding seagrass decline near coastal development

A seagrass meadow near a growing coastal town is declining. Local people report muddier water after rainfall. There is new shoreline development, road construction and increased runoff from the surrounding catchment.

## The narrow question

> Is the seagrass cover declining?

This is important, but too narrow.

## The better question

> Are land-use change, runoff, sedimentation or coastal development affecting the meadow, and are governance systems able to respond?

::{.callout-note title="Scientific interpretation"}
Seagrass condition is often shaped by cross-scale drivers. Settings and governance indicators help reveal pressures that are not visible if monitoring focuses only inside the meadow boundary.
:::

## Example monitoring plan

| Domain | What to monitor | Example indicator | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Settings | Land-sea pressures | Land use, runoff, river proximity, development pressure | Identifies external drivers affecting the meadow |
| Resource system | Meadow condition | Cover, extent, epiphytes, water clarity | Shows ecological response to changing conditions |
| Resource units | Ecological function | Fish, invertebrates, nursery indicators | Shows whether decline affects biodiversity and resources |
| Actors | Affected and influential groups | Fishers, residents, developers, regulators, tourism operators | Identifies who depends on or influences the system |
| Governance system | Planning and water quality governance | Development rules, permitting, enforcement, agency coordination | Determines whether land-based drivers can be managed |
| Interactions | Pressure pathways | Runoff events, dredging, shoreline modification, community reporting | Connects external activities with meadow condition |
| Outcomes | Management response and recovery | Meadow trajectory, resource change, policy response, perceived fairness | Shows whether monitoring leads to action |

## What the results might show

### Seagrass declines after major runoff events

Sedimentation or water quality may be a key pressure.

### Fishers report declining resources before surveys detect major cover loss

Local knowledge may provide early warning.

### Several agencies have partial responsibility but none has clear responsibility for seagrass

Governance fragmentation may be a central barrier.

### Development continues despite evidence of pressure

Monitoring needs to connect more strongly with planning and policy processes.

::{.callout-tip title="Key message"}
Some seagrass problems begin outside the meadow. Monitoring must connect land, water, people, governance and ecological change.
:::
